analogy: Think of "Weavers Iron Smelters and Factory Owners" through the chapter's own opening images: Civilising the “Native”, Educating the Nation 6 In the earlier chapters, you have seen how British rule affected rajas and nawabs, peasants and tribals. In this chapter, we will try and understand what implication it had for the lives of students. For, the British in... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Weavers Iron Smelters and Factory Owners is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that civilising the “Native”, Educating the Nation 6 In the earlier chapters, you have seen how British rule affected rajas and nawabs, peasants and tribals.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will try and understand what implication it had for the lives of students.

The text explains that for, the British in India wanted not only territorial conquest and control over revenues.

The text explains that they also felt that they had a cultural mission: they had to “civilise the natives”, change their customs and values.

The text explains that how were Indians to be educated, “civilised”, and made into what the British believed were “good subjects”?

The text explains that the British could find no simple answers to these questions.

The text explains that they continued to be debated for many decades.

The text explains that how the British saw Education Let us look at what the British thought and did, and how some of the ideas of education that we now take for granted evolved in the last two hundred years.

The text explains that in the process of this enquiry, we will also see how Indians reacted to British ideas, and how they developed their own views about how Indians were to be educated.

The text explains that the tradition of Orientalism In 1783, a person named William Jones arrived in Calcutta.

The text explains that he had an appointment as a junior judge at the Supreme Court that the Company had set up.

The text explains that in addition to being an expert in law, Jones was a linguist.

The text explains that he had studied Greek and Latin at Oxford, knew French and English, had picked up Arabic from a friend, and had also learnt Persian.

The text explains that at Calcutta, he began spending many hours a day with pandits who taught him the subtleties of Sanskrit language, grammar and Fig.

The text explains that 1 – William Jones learning Persian Linguist – Someone who knows and studies several languages

The text explains that 66 OUR PASTS – III Madrasa – An Arabic word for a place of learning; any type of school or college poetry.

The text explains that education for commerce In 1854, the Court of Directors of the East India Company in London sent an educational despatch to the Governor-General in India.

The text explains that european learning, it said, would enable Indians to recognise the advantages that flow from the expansion of trade and commerce, and make them see the importance of developing the resources of the country.

The text explains that from Thomas Babington Macaulay, Minute of 2 February 1835 on Indian Education Source 1

The text explains that after 1857, however, the British government in India was reluctant to directly support missionary education.

The text explains that (c) Mahatma Gandhi thought that promotion of literacy was the most important aim of education.

The text explains that (d) Rabindranath Tagore felt that children ought to be subjected to strict discipline.

The text explains that why did William Jones feel the need to study Indian history, philosophy and law?

The text explains that why did James Mill and Thomas Macaulay think that European education was essential in India?

The text explains that why did Mahatma Gandhi want to teach children handicrafts?

The text explains that why did Mahatma Gandhi think that English education had enslaved Indians?

The text explains that let’s imagine Imagine you were witness to a debate between Mahatma Gandhi and Macaulay on English education.

The text explains that cIVILISING THE “NATIVE”, EDUCATING THE NATION 77 Let’s do 7.

The text explains that find out from your grandparents about what they studied in school.

The text explains that find out about the history of your school or any other school in the area you live.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III, Chapter 6: Weavers Iron Smelters and Factory Owners

schemes.point: Following Macaulay’s minute, the English Education Act of 1835 was introduced. (NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III Ch6 pg 3)

schemes.point: Education departments of the government were set up to extend control over all matters regarding education. (NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III Ch6 pg 4)

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Weavers Iron Smelters and Factory Owners, which statement is supported by the source text about 70 OUR PASTS – III?
options: Jones discovered that his interests were shared by many British officials living in Calcutta at the time.
Englishmen like Henry Thomas Colebrooke and Nathaniel Halhed were also busy discovering the ancient Indian heritage, mastering Indian languages and translating Sanskrit and Persian...
Together with them, Jones set up the Asiatic Society of Bengal, and started a journal called Asiatick Researches.
Jones and Colebrooke came to represent a particular attitude towards India.
